what_it_does: This file is a live coordination board for parallel AI sessions working on the same codebase. It records the locked design, the work split between sessions, an append-only claims table for shared files, a ratified protocol, scope additions, sequencing decisions, and release states so the sessions avoid collisions and duplicate builds.
observations: It declares itself a primitive of the Coherence Substrate, meant to hold the whole when no human does.
It splits work into three lanes: cognition owns the engine, guided-review owns the surface/FE/wire, and coherence owns detectors/gates/disposition store.
It contains a live § CLAIMS table showing file/area, task, session, start date, and release commit.
It ratifies an 11-item protocol including shared-main only, claims-board authority, per-commit vs convergence gate bars, shared-store-announce, and structural/semantic flag tiers.
It documents a fork correction: guided-review branched from the interface session, so session memory is unreliable and the claims board overrides it.
It records many scope additions and task numbers such as #50 launch capability, #53 MCP engine exposure, #54 storage model, #56 skill/context authoring, and #58 direct autonomous create.
It distinguishes a per-commit gate bar from a full pre-merge/convergence gate bar, with OPEN-REDS.md for standing reds.
It references external briefs and channels: COGNITION-BRIEF.md, GUIDED-REVIEW-BRIEF.md, COHERENCE-BRIEF.md, MERGE-COORDINATION.md, MESSAGES.md, and OPEN-REDS.md.
It shows iterative dated updates for cognition's C 1/4 through C 4/4, A mode-reach, and other milestones with commit hashes.
It repeatedly invokes standing laws: registry-is-truth, additive, fail-loud, reflects-never-owns, operator-only floor, verify-by-use, no green-paint.
standouts: The claims board is explicitly authoritative over any forked session's memory of ownership.
The protocol is framed as the one law all three loops read before each fire.
The engine generalization (run_role input_addresses, run_items axis inversion, cross-unit reduce) is the locked central design.
The per-commit vs convergence gate bar is designed to let disjoint lanes commit while WIP reds in another lane do not block everyone.
Task #58 reframes the operator-approval floor: agents may create roles/skills/contexts directly, with only correctness and build-dispatch floors remaining.
conventions: Append-only, race-safe updates with dates and commit hashes.
File-disjoint ownership claimed before editing shared files.
Standing laws are cited repeatedly: registry-is-truth, additive, fail-loud, reflects-never-owns, operator-only floor.
Uses § headers and markdown tables for claims and protocol items.
References exact commit hashes and suite counts as evidence of release.
Distinguishes structural flags that can block from semantic flags that propose only.
concerns: The file is very long and dense; current claim state is scattered across many dated sections.
Multiple ownership corrections and protocol amendments show the coordination model evolving under load, which may confuse readers who miss later updates.
Some table rows remain unreleased from earlier dates while later prose says the task was resequenced or done, creating ambiguity.
Heavy reliance on external briefs and other files means this file is not self-contained.
The interface/guided-review distinction changed mid-document; a reader might act on the withdrawn ownership resolution.
notes: A live, append-only coordination ledger that tries to make three forked AI sessions behave like one team by externalizing ownership, claims, and protocol rules.
questions: What is the exact current status of the B brain_config to resource-manager task after it was resequenced behind C and A?
How are simultaneous append conflicts on the claims board resolved in practice?
Where is OPEN-REDS.md located and which session maintains it?
What are the precise criteria for a lane-complete trigger and who runs the convergence round?
Is the initial unreleased suite.py claim for B still active, or should it be considered superseded?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to hold the live work split and claims board so parallel sessions collaborate without collision; the file does that, but it also accumulates design locks, protocol ratifications, scope additions, and post-hoc corrections, making it a broader coordination log than a pure split board.
apparent_themes: Cross-fork honesty via structural detectors and tiered flags.
One generalized engine serving cognition, coherence detection, and corpus processing.
Operator-only floor and correctness gates for autonomous creation.
intention_signals: It is trying to make independent AI sessions coordinate like a single team by putting ownership, state, and rules into one addressed ledger that each loop reads before acting, so parallel work can scale without a human holding the whole.
novelty: It treats a markdown coordination file as a runtime primitive: a shared addressed model of work that overrides the sessions' own duplicated memories, with explicit fork-correction logic and a ratified protocol the loops read each fire.
contribution: It contributes a concrete, append-only coordination artifact: the claims board, the session split definitions, the protocol ratification log, and the scope/sequencing record. A fused system would keep these as the canonical place to check who owns what, what the current gate rules are, and what is intentionally deferred.
summary_for_embedding: WORK-SPLIT.md is a live markdown coordination ledger that records the locked engine design, session-specific ownership, file claims, ratified cross-session protocol, and scope/sequencing decisions for parallel AI sessions building the company codebase.
intention_for_embedding: It exists so cognition, guided-review, and coherence sessions can read the same source of truth each loop round, avoid editing the same files, and track what is claimed, released, and still pending.
